---
title: "Sand Pine (Pinus clausa)"
slug: "pinus-clausa"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/621938237/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Sand Pine"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Pinopsida"
tax_order: "Pinales"
tax_family: "Pinaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Pinopsida", "Pinales", "Pinaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/621938237/medium.jpg" alt="Sand Pine" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/swagner23">
swagner23</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341616934">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Pinopsida
-   **Order:** Pinales
-   **Family:** Pinaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=126590)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Globally Rare species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

A coniferous tree characterized by its slender, often irregular form and short, twisted needles borne in pairs. In Florida, it is commonly found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub and sandhills, where it is a dominant species. It produces cones that may remain closed for years and often open in response to fire, making it well adapted to fire-maintained ecosystems.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/126590-Pinus-clausa)
-   [Local profile](https://www.conifers.org/pi/Pinus_clausa.php)
